# ADR-260036: Network View Outer-Circumference Edge Initiation and Selection Affordance

## Workflow

- This ADR records the accepted pointer-interaction refinement for Network view circle nodes.
- It replaces side connector-dot edge initiation with an outer-circumference interaction ring.
- It preserves circle-node representation and closest-boundary edge anchoring semantics from prior accepted decisions.

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-05-13
Owners: Repository owner, product collaborator
Supersedes: ADR-260029 (D2 only)

## Context

Network view currently uses a visible side connector dot as the relationship drag affordance. The product collaborator requested a cleaner interaction model where users initiate relationship drag from a full outer ring while preserving easy node dragging and selection.

The model must keep these behaviors explicit:

- Inner node body remains draggable for node movement.
- Outer ring advertises selectability and edge-creation affordance.
- Drop on blank space performs no action.
- Drop on source node performs no action (self-bonds disallowed).
- Drop on another atom circle creates a bond.

## Decision

### D1: Full-Ring Affordance Replaces Connector Dot
- Network view removes the side connector dot.
- A full 360-degree outer circumference ring is the edge-initiation affordance.

### D2: Ring Visibility and State Rules
- The ring is visible on hover and when the atom is selected.
- During edge drag, a valid target atom reuses the same ring highlight semantics (no separate visual language).

### D3: Pointer-Zone Split
- Inner circle body remains the primary drag surface for moving atoms.
- Outer ring starts edge drag gestures.
- Clicking the ring without dragging still selects the atom.

### D4: Connection Completion and Cancellation Semantics
- Releasing a drag gesture over blank canvas is a no-op.
- Releasing over the source atom is a no-op.
- Releasing over another atom circle completes the connection flow.

### D5: Scope Boundary
- This interaction grammar applies to Network view only.
- Flow-view interaction model and underlying atom/bond data contracts remain unchanged.
- Desktop keyboard/mouse is the primary input target for this slice; touch-parity refinement is deferred under desktop-only MVP scope.

## Alternatives Considered

- Option A: Keep connector dot and only add hover ring. Rejected: does not remove mixed affordance complexity.
- Option B: Ring visible at all times. Rejected: adds persistent visual noise in dense graphs.
- Option C: Require drop on target ring only. Rejected: lower usability than accepting drop over full target circle.

## Consequences

### Positive
- More discoverable and consistent relationship gesture model across all angles.
- Cleaner node visual language without separate connector dot.
- Better parity between hover, selected, and drag-target states.

### Negative
- Higher implementation sensitivity to pointer-zone hit-testing.
- Potential accidental edge-start gestures if ring width is too large.

### Risks
- Ring hit area that is too narrow may feel unreliable on dense canvases.
- Ring hit area that is too wide may interfere with node drag intent.
- Pointer behavior may differ across mouse vs touch input.

## Verification

- Verify connector dot is absent in Network view.
- Verify ring appears on hover and selected states.
- Verify ring click selects node when no drag occurs.
- Verify inner body drag still moves nodes.
- Verify edge drag can start from any angle on ring.
- Verify edge drop on blank/source is no-op.
- Verify edge drop on another atom circle opens normal bond-creation flow.
- Verify Flow view behavior remains unchanged.

## Rollback

- Restore connector-dot initiation if pointer-zone conflicts cause unacceptable drag ambiguity.
- Keep circle-node and boundary-anchor rendering even if ring gesture is reverted.
